question: Your app tier runs in eu-west-1 and your database is still in us-east-1 (75 ms round trip). A request makes 6 sequential queries that each took 0.55 ms when everything was in one region. What happens to p50 latency?
options: Roughly unchanged — the queries themselves are just as fast, and the network is only a few milliseconds
It roughly doubles, because the network adds about as much as the query work
It goes from about 9 ms to about 456 ms, because 6 sequential round trips is 450 ms of pure propagation
It gets worse only at p99; the median is protected because most requests hit the connection pool
explanation: Six sequential queries pay six round trips: 6 x 75 ms = 450 ms of propagation before any work happens. The measured run went from 9.3 ms to 456.2 ms p50 — 49.3x. The tempting wrong answers assume the network is a small additive term, but for sequential dependent calls it multiplies by the number of round trips. Note the giveaway in the real data: remote p50 (456.2 ms) and p99 (467.0 ms) are nearly identical, because a fixed propagation cost is not a tail phenomenon — no percentile escapes it.

question: You lower your DNS record's TTL to 60 seconds so failover will be quick. One hour after a health-check-driven failover, what fraction of clients is still resolving to the dead region?
options: 0% — the TTL expired 59 minutes ago
About 1%, entirely explained by clock skew between resolvers
About 5%, because resolver TTL floors, pooled HTTP clients, JVM caching and hard-coded addresses all ignore your TTL
About 50%, because DNS caches are refreshed only on process restart
explanation: The measured residual was 5.24% at one hour, and the curve never fell below 1% within 24 hours. RFC 2181 section 8 makes the TTL an upper bound on caching — nothing enforces a lower one. The modelled populations are 62% honouring the 60 s TTL, 22% behind a 300 s resolver floor, 10% pooled HTTP clients at 30 minutes, 5% JVMs with networkaddress.cache.ttl = -1 (forever), and 1% with the address in a config file. Answer A is the assumption the configuration invites you to make; it is wrong because the TTL binds your authoritative server, not other people's caches.

question: Two regions accept writes to the same entity during a partition, and the database resolves with last-write-wins on wall-clock timestamps. What is the most dangerous property of that scheme?
options: It is slow, because comparing timestamps across regions requires a cross-region round trip
It discards acknowledged writes silently, and which write 'wins' depends on clock skew you cannot audit afterwards
It always keeps both versions, so the application must merge them
It rejects the second write, so clients see errors during every partition
explanation: LWW is not 'the last write wins' — it is 'the write carrying the largest number wins', and that number comes from a clock. The measured run discarded 694 acknowledged writes with zero errors returned to anyone. Vary only the clock and correctness moves: 0 inversions with perfect clocks, 1 (0.5%) at an ordinary 250 ms NTP offset, and 27 of 209 resolutions (12.9%) keeping the EARLIER write at 10 s of drift. Option C describes version vectors and option D describes home-region pinning — both are honest about what they do; LWW's problem is precisely that it is not.

question: You compare active-passive, active-active with one write region, regional data ownership, and full multi-master. Which statement matches the measured year-long simulation?
options: Active-passive is the cheapest, because the standby can be smaller than the primary
All four cost the same 2.0x compute; what differs is latency, RTO, conflict exposure and whether the second region's capacity does anything
Multi-master is strictly best — it had the lowest write latency and the highest availability
Regional data ownership costs the most, because pinning requires extra replication
explanation: The cost column was flat at 2.0x for all four, because surviving the loss of 1 of 2 regions means each region must hold 100% of demand — that is arithmetic, not a topology choice. What differs: active-passive had the worst p99 read (93.6 ms), the worst RTO (34 min), and 50% of its spend idle; regional ownership reached 19.6 ms / 95.6 ms / 9 min / 99.993% with no conflict exposure. Option C is the trap: multi-master did win every latency column (28.7 ms p99 writes, 99.995%), and it is the only one of the four that produces write conflicts — a cost that appears in no latency table.

question: Two identical evacuations of a dead region: one survivor was running at 90% utilization, the other at 50%. Both saw the same 50.0% peak error rate. What did the headroom actually buy?
options: A lower peak error rate, because the extra capacity absorbs the initial spike
Faster detection, because a less loaded region notices failures sooner
A shorter outage: full recovery in 55 s instead of 407 s, and 455,000 failed requests instead of 2,941,245
Nothing measurable — both runs recovered at the same time once autoscaling completed
explanation: The identical 50.0% peak is the 35-second detection window, and headroom does nothing about it — detection lag costs everyone equally. The difference appears after redirection completes: the provisioned survivor dropped to 0.0% errors and was fully recovered at 55 s, while the tight one sat at 44.4% errors for another six minutes waiting for autoscaling and did not recover until 407 s. That is 6.5x the failed requests. Headroom does not shorten detection; it shortens the outage — which is why the rule is to run at most (N-1)/N utilization, or 50% for two regions.

question: Your business says 'we can afford to lose no data at all — set RPO to zero.' What must you tell them?
options: That RPO is a property of the topology, so switching from active-passive to active-active will fix it
That RPO zero requires a synchronous cross-region commit, adding roughly a full cross-region round trip (+75 ms) to the p50 of every write, forever
That RPO zero is free once replication is streaming rather than batched
That RPO zero is already achieved, because the measured p50 replication lag was only 84 ms
explanation: Measured: async writes had p50 17.6 ms; synchronous cross-region commit had p50 92.4 ms — exactly one cross-region round trip added to every write, including the 364 days a year when nothing fails. Option A is a common and expensive misconception: RPO was identical (7.30 s) across all four topologies, because RPO belongs to your replication mechanism, not your topology. Option D confuses the median with the tail — the p99 lag was 7.30 s and the max 9.10 s, and a region does not politely fail at the median, so you quote the tail.
